articles.title: Virginia voters approve redistricting push as courts race to decide whether the map can stand
articles.summary: A referendum that could reshape Virginia’s congressional map in time for the 2026 midterms now collides with a still-unresolved state Supreme Court case, adding a fresh front to the national redistricting war.

articles.title: As public media money disappears, local stations are being forced to choose what culture means
articles.summary: The collapse of federal support for public broadcasting is not just a media policy story. It is a culture story about who gets to preserve archives, fund children’s programming, record local music, cover festivals, and keep noncommercial audiences connected to civic life.

articles.summary: Utilities, regulators, and tech giants are racing to keep up with a surge in AI computing that is colliding with transmission bottlenecks, equipment shortages, and a new politics of power costs.

articles.title: March inflation spike rekindles the housing-and-gas budget squeeze
articles.summary: A fresh surge in gasoline and shelter costs is complicating the Fed’s path, squeezing paychecks, and reordering the spring housing market just as buyers hoped for a thaw.

articles.title: How the federal student-loan reset is rippling through colleges, households and public budgets
articles.summary: The Trump administration’s push to move more student-loan operations toward the Treasury Department is more than a bureaucratic shakeup: it is colliding with renewed collection activity, the unwinding of the SAVE repayment plan and a higher-education funding environment in which state support is rising more slowly than enrollment at many public institutions.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: How the federal student-loan reset is rippling through colleges, households and public budgets. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Measles resurgence tests the U.S. public-health firewall as outbreaks, misinformation and policy churn collide
articles.summary: A record-since-elimination year for measles has exposed how quickly vaccine gaps, travel importations, weakened trust and uneven surveillance can turn a preventable disease into a systems failure.

articles.title: The lonely age of artificial friends
articles.summary: AI companion chatbots are no longer a novelty; they are a policy problem, a mental-health question, and a philosophical test of what we owe one another when machines learn to simulate care.

articles.title: NASA’s moonshot just came back: Artemis II ends on a clean arc, but the harder science begins now
articles.summary: Artemis II’s April 2026 splashdown gave NASA, ESA, and their contractors the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years and a real-world test of Orion, its European Service Module, and recovery operations. The mission was a major technical success — but it is only the start of the work needed to make lunar transportation routine, affordable, and scientifically useful.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: NASA’s moonshot just came back: Artemis II ends on a clean arc, but the harder science begins now. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Lebanon’s war spillover is redrawing the Syria border and testing the region’s aid architecture
articles.summary: A fresh border crisis has turned Lebanon into the latest pressure point in the Middle East war, pushing Syrians and Lebanese civilians across a frontier that is also a lifeline for trade, health care and returns to a still-fragile Syria.

articles.title: America Should Stop Pretending Data Centers Are Just Warehouses With Servers
articles.summary: The AI boom is colliding with the power grid in real time. The right response is not a blanket moratorium, but a hard new rule: if a data center wants scarce grid access, it must prove it can pay its full system cost, flex its load, and tell the truth about its energy use.
articles.image_credit: AI-generated editorial thumbnail for: America Should Stop Pretending Data Centers Are Just Warehouses With Servers. AI-generated image by The Press.

articles.title: Birders Are Becoming Citizen Scientists in the Age of Avian Flu
articles.summary: A once-hobbyist ritual—counting birds in backyards, parks and migration corridors—has become a live data network for conservation, disease tracking and community identity, even as bird flu and digital platforms reshape who participates and what gets counted.
